title: Por que a democracia não funciona para todos: estatuto social e apoio à democracia na Europa

section_reading_log.section_summary: The introduction frames the article around dissatisfaction with democracy in established democracies and asks why socioeconomic status affects support for democracy. It argues that prior evidence shows higher-status citizens tend to support democracy more, but that the mechanism behind this relationship remains underexplained. The article proposes to analyze how perceived social position shapes democratic expectations and evaluations, and whether this varies across European country groups.
section_reading_log.methods_or_data_mentions: The section states that the analysis uses data from 26 countries from the European Social Survey Round 6 and tests country-level variance across post-communist countries and countries affected by the Great Recession. It previews an empirical analysis of subjective social status, income, education, and occupation.
section_reading_log.classification_relevance: This section establishes that the article is empirical and quantitative: it will test hypotheses using survey data across 26 countries. It also introduces causal/explanatory language about how status affects support for democracy, but the design is observational rather than an explicit credibility-revolution design.

section_reading_log.section_summary: This is an organizing section that introduces the conceptual discussion of democratic support. It signals that the paper will distinguish different forms of support for democracy rather than treating democratic support as a single attitude.
section_reading_log.methods_or_data_mentions: No direct empirical data or method is presented in this heading itself; the substantive conceptual and measurement discussion appears in the following subsections.
section_reading_log.classification_relevance: The section matters mainly for theory and measurement. It does not independently affect the empirical classification, but it prepares the multidimensional dependent variables used later.

section_reading_log.section_summary: This subsection distinguishes democratic expectations from democratic evaluations. Expectations are defined as diffuse, normative views about what democracy should be, while evaluations concern perceived performance of the democratic regime in the respondent's country. The section argues that evaluations should be analyzed relative to citizens' ideals because legitimacy depends on the gap between democratic norms and perceived reality.
section_reading_log.methods_or_data_mentions: The section discusses conceptual measurement and cites previous work by Ferrin and Kriesi, Wessels, Easton, and others. It does not present original data analysis here, but it defines the conceptual basis for the dependent variables.
section_reading_log.classification_relevance: This section is relevant to the article's empirical model because it defines two outcome families: democratic expectations and democratic evaluations. It contains no original quantitative estimates, qualitative evidence, or causal design by itself.

section_reading_log.section_title: Support for which kind of democracy?
section_reading_log.section_summary: This subsection argues that democracy is multidimensional and that citizens, like theorists, may support different models of democracy. It contrasts liberal/procedural, participatory, and social/substantive understandings of democracy, then states that the article identifies liberal democracy and social democracy as two main dimensions of citizens' democratic support. Direct democracy is added as a robustness dimension.
section_reading_log.methods_or_data_mentions: The section explicitly reports an exploratory factor analytical approach using ESS Round 6 items on democratic expectations and evaluations. It states that EFA identifies two main dimensions, liberal democracy and social democracy, with details in Appendix B. This is original quantitative measurement work.
section_reading_log.classification_relevance: This section supports classification as quantitative and statistical modeling because exploratory factor analysis is used analytically to construct dimensions. It also shows that the article reanalyzes survey data rather than merely citing external findings.

section_reading_log.section_summary: This is an organizing section that introduces the theoretical argument about social status. It marks the transition from conceptualizing democratic support to explaining why social position should shape expectations and evaluations.
section_reading_log.methods_or_data_mentions: No data analysis is presented in this heading itself. The detailed hypotheses and theoretical mechanisms appear in the following subsections.
section_reading_log.classification_relevance: The heading itself does not alter the empirical classification, but the following subsections develop the explanatory claims that later connect quantitative models to causal or mechanism-oriented arguments.

section_reading_log.section_title: Procedure vs. substance: How social status affects democratic support
section_reading_log.section_summary: This subsection develops the article's main individual-level theory. Drawing on social dominance theory, social comparison theory, relative deprivation, and the resource model of participation, it argues that high-status citizens are more likely to support liberal/procedural democracy because they benefit from the status quo, while low-status citizens are more likely to demand substantive social-democratic outputs and evaluate their own democracy more negatively. It presents hypotheses H1, H2, and H3 on perceived status, procedural expectations, substantive expectations, and democratic evaluations.
section_reading_log.methods_or_data_mentions: The section contains no original data analysis, but it specifies testable hypotheses. It mentions Figure 1 as an illustration of the expected relationship between perceived status and democratic support.
section_reading_log.classification_relevance: This section is central for the causal/explanatory classification. The article explicitly claims that perceived social status influences democratic expectations and evaluations. However, this is a theory-driven observational claim, not an experimental, DiD, IV, RDD, matching, or similar causal identification design.

section_reading_log.section_title: Social status in Europe: Where do the dissatisfied live?
section_reading_log.section_summary: This subsection develops country-level hypotheses about whether the effects of status differ across European contexts. It argues that post-communist countries may emphasize democratic outputs because of authoritarian and communist socialization, and that Eurozone crisis countries may make socioeconomic status more politically salient because of economic hardship and austerity. The section presents H4 and H5, predicting stronger status effects in post-communist and crisis-hit countries.
section_reading_log.methods_or_data_mentions: The section does not present original estimates, but it defines the comparative country-group framework used later in the empirical models. It refers to prior research on post-communist socialization, democratic legitimacy, economic crisis, and democratic support.
section_reading_log.classification_relevance: This section strengthens the explanatory and causal language of the paper by predicting cross-level moderation. It prepares the later multilevel interaction models but contains no credibility-revolution design on its own.

section_reading_log.section_summary: This section describes the empirical data, variables, and model specification. Individual-level data come from ESS Round 6, while country-level indicators come from the Democracy Barometer and the Standardized World Income Inequality Database. The dependent variables are additive indices for liberal, social, and direct democratic expectations and evaluations. The main explanatory variable is subjective social status, measured by respondents' place in society, with income, education, and occupation used as objective status indicators.
section_reading_log.methods_or_data_mentions: The section states that all models are linear hierarchical mixed-effects models with respondents nested in countries, random intercepts, and random slopes for social status in cross-level interaction models. It reports use of ESS post-stratification and population size weights and robustness tests without population size weights. It also defines post-communist and Eurozone crisis country groups and individual- and country-level controls.
section_reading_log.classification_relevance: This is the strongest evidence for quantitative statistical modeling. It establishes original or reanalyzed quantitative survey analysis, multilevel modeling, cross-level interactions, controls, and weighting. It supports statistical-modeling classification and makes the credibility-revolution screen applicable, although no modern causal identification design is described.

section_reading_log.section_summary: This is an organizing section for the empirical findings. It introduces the division between individual-level effects and country-level effects.
section_reading_log.methods_or_data_mentions: No separate empirical estimates are presented under this heading alone; the results are reported in the following subsections using figures, models, marginal effects, and appendices.
section_reading_log.classification_relevance: The heading itself does not add classification evidence, but it marks the beginning of the original quantitative results.

section_reading_log.section_summary: This subsection reports the individual-level multilevel regression results. It finds that subjective status has the expected effects: higher perceived status is associated with higher liberal democratic expectations and lower social democratic expectations, confirming H1 and H2. Objective socioeconomic indicators show similar but smaller effects. The section also reports that higher subjective status improves evaluations of both liberal and social democracy, supporting H3, while objective indicators show more mixed results.
section_reading_log.methods_or_data_mentions: The section discusses coefficient plots from multilevel regression models in Figures 2 and 3, with robustness models for direct democracy in Appendix tables. It refers to effects of subjective status, income, education, occupation, and gender. The language reports modeled effects rather than descriptive summaries only.
section_reading_log.classification_relevance: This section confirms that the article conducts original quantitative statistical modeling and interprets model coefficients. It reports inferential language about effects and hypothesis confirmation, supporting quantitative classification and the presence of statistical inference.

section_reading_log.section_summary: This subsection reports cross-level interaction results for country groups and subjective status. It finds that H4 and H5 are mostly not supported for democratic expectations: status effects are weaker in post-communist and Eurocrisis countries than predicted, while expectations are generally higher in those country groups. For evaluations, the article finds partial support for H4 on liberal democratic evaluations in post-communist countries but contradicts H5 for Eurocrisis countries. It also interprets differences in predicted levels and robustness tests for direct democracy.
section_reading_log.methods_or_data_mentions: The section uses cross-level interaction terms, marginal effect plots, predicted levels, and appendix models. It reports significant differences, non-significant effects, and robustness checks with direct democratic expectations and evaluations.
section_reading_log.classification_relevance: This section provides further evidence of statistical modeling and statistical inference through interaction models, marginal effects, significance language, and robustness tests. It does not present a credibility-revolution causal design; the evidence is observational multilevel regression with explanatory claims.

section_reading_log.section_summary: The conclusion summarizes the article's findings: perceived social status systematically shapes democratic expectations and evaluations. Low-status citizens tend to prefer substantive over procedural democracy and evaluate their own democratic systems more negatively, creating larger democratic deficits. Contrary to expectations, status matters more in Western Europe, while post-communist and Eurocrisis countries show higher expectations and lower evaluations across status groups. The article concludes that dissatisfaction with democracy often reflects disappointment with social justice and redistribution rather than rejection of democracy as a principle.
section_reading_log.methods_or_data_mentions: The conclusion interprets the quantitative results and returns to the theoretical implications. It does not introduce new data or methods but summarizes findings from the multilevel models and country-group comparisons.
section_reading_log.classification_relevance: The conclusion reinforces the article's explanatory claims about status and democratic support. It confirms that the empirical contribution is quantitative and model-based, with no qualitative evidence and no explicit credibility-revolution method.

general_summary:
The article studies how perceived and objective social status shape citizens' support for democracy in Europe. It argues that democratic support should be separated into expectations and evaluations, and into liberal/procedural and social/substantive dimensions. Using ESS Round 6 and country-level data, it finds that higher perceived status is associated with stronger liberal democratic expectations and more positive evaluations, while lower perceived status is associated with stronger social-democratic expectations and more critical evaluations. The paper also tests whether these relationships differ in post-communist and Eurozone crisis countries, finding that the predicted stronger status effects in those contexts are mostly not supported.

Methodologically, the article is an empirical quantitative paper based on survey and country-level data. It uses exploratory factor analysis to identify dimensions of democratic support and hierarchical mixed-effects regression models with cross-level interactions to test hypotheses. It reports model-based effects, marginal effects, predicted levels, significance language, and robustness tests, but it does not use an experiment, difference-in-differences, instrumental variables, regression discontinuity, matching, synthetic control, or another credibility-revolution design.

decision_audit.own_empirical_evidence: The article presents its own empirical evidence in the Data and operationalization section and the Results subsections, using ESS Round 6 individual-level data for 26 countries plus country-level data from the Democracy Barometer and SWIID.
decision_audit.own_quantitative_analysis: Original or reanalyzed quantitative analysis appears in the EFA used to identify democratic-support dimensions, the additive indices, and the hierarchical mixed-effects models reported in the Results section and appendices.
decision_audit.qualitative_evidence: The article does not present substantive qualitative evidence such as interviews, archival analysis, case-study process tracing, discourse analysis, or qualitative content analysis. Its theory sections cite literature but do not analyze qualitative empirical material.
decision_audit.statistical_inference: Statistical inference appears in the regression results and interaction analyses, including references to significant differences, non-significant effects, coefficient plots, marginal effects, and model tables in the appendix.
decision_audit.causal_or_credibility_design: The article makes explanatory claims that social status affects democratic expectations and evaluations, but it uses observational multilevel regression rather than a credibility-revolution design such as an experiment, DiD, IV, RDD, synthetic control, matching, or weighting for causal identification.
decision_audit.contradictory_sections: No section contradicts the classification as an empirical quantitative statistical-modeling paper. The theoretical sections are extensive, but the Data and Results sections clearly present original quantitative analysis.

classification.title: Por que a democracia não funciona para todos: estatuto social e apoio à democracia na Europa
classification.quantitative_analysis_evidence_quote: All models are linear, hierarchical, mixed-effects models, with respondents nested in countries.
classification.statistical_inference_quote: there are indeed significant differences between country groups in the strength of the effect of status on expectations for liberal democracy
classification.causal_or_explanatory_claim_quote: I assume that the perceived social position of citizens influences both their expectations and evaluations of democracy.
classification.causal_design_quote: All models are linear, hierarchical, mixed-effects models, with respondents nested in countries.
classification.main_variables_or_relationship: The article analyzes how perceived social status, income, education, and occupation relate to democratic expectations and evaluations across liberal, social, and direct democratic dimensions, including variation across post-communist, Eurozone crisis, and Western European country groups.
classification.sample_or_data_source: European Social Survey Round 6 individual-level data for 26 countries, combined with country-level data from the Democracy Barometer and the Standardized World Income Inequality Database.
classification.brief_justification: The full body shows an empirical quantitative article using ESS survey data, EFA, and hierarchical mixed-effects models to test explanatory hypotheses about social status and democratic support. It has statistical inference and causal/explanatory language, but no explicit credibility-revolution identification design; the causal method is best characterized as observational regression with causal claim and no design.
